The core concept explored is leveraging femoral arterial Doppler ultrasound waveforms obtained *during* active compressions to detect signs of Return of Spontaneous Circulation (ROSC). Instead of pausing chest compressions every two minutes for a traditional pulse check, the authors evaluate if detecting pulsatility and anterograde flow signals can reliably predict true cardiac activity. If validated, this represents a significant workflow improvement by maintaining continuous, uninterrupted high-quality CPR monitoring, which is paramount for optimizing resuscitation outcomes.

articles.title: Patterns of emergency department visits for suicidal attempts associated with toxic ingestion: A retrospective cross-sectional study
articles.takeaway: When managing a patient presenting with intentional overdose in the ED, anticipate polypharmacy involving CNS depressants as common agents. Given this complexity, prompt toxicology consultation is non-negotiable for guiding antidotal therapy and monitoring for multi-system effects. Remember that optimizing care involves coordinating both acute stabilization and risk assessment.
articles.shortSummary: This retrospective analysis characterizing intentional self-poisoning presentations in Israel highlights a pattern where medication overdose is the most common etiology, frequently involving polypharmacy, particularly w…
articles.summary: This retrospective analysis characterizing intentional self-poisoning presentations in Israel highlights a pattern where medication overdose is the most common etiology, frequently involving polypharmacy, particularly with central nervous system depressants. The authors emphasize that managing these complex ingestions requires more than just standard clinical care; early and robust toxicology consultation is crucial for guiding definitive treatment pathways and improving patient outcomes. Furthermore, the report underscores the broader role of medical toxicologists, extending their purview beyond immediate stabilization to include surveillance, prevention efforts, and addressing the psychological aspects of self-harm risk.

articles.title: Status epilepticus in patients with known epilepsy presenting to the emergency department: Predictors and mortality risk
articles.takeaway: Given the significant association between SE in this population and increased mortality, consider systematically assessing all incoming epilepsy patients for underlying triggers. Pay extra attention to those who are elderly, have known metabolic issues, or present with signs suggestive of CNS infection, as these groups warrant heightened suspicion for impending or ongoing seizures. Early risk stratification is key here.
articles.shortSummary: This review looked at the prevalence and predictors of status epilepticus (SE) specifically within a cohort of epilepsy patients presenting to the emergency department in Malaysia.
articles.summary: This review looked at the prevalence and predictors of status epilepticus (SE) specifically within a cohort of epilepsy patients presenting to the emergency department in Malaysia. The authors found that SE was quite common, occurring in nearly one-quarter of this population, and critically, it was strongly associated with elevated in-hospital mortality rates. This suggests that simply having an epilepsy diagnosis does not preclude the risk of severe status epilepticus upon presentation. The findings emphasize the utility of identifying high-risk subgroups for proactive management. Specifically, the data point toward older age, metabolic derangements, or suspicion of a central nervous system infection as key areas for enhanced vigilance and early risk stratification.

articles.title: Risk of obstructive acute kidney injury: derivation and internal validation of a risk stratification tree
articles.takeaway: When managing AKI in the ED, consider using this framework to triage suspicion for obstruction; acute flank/lumbar pain strongly suggests a higher pre-test probability. Remember that while the model shows high sensitivity for 'low risk,' it is based on internal validation and requires external testing before changing standard diagnostic pathways. Clinicians should interpret these scores as adjuncts to physical exam findings, not replacements.
articles.shortSummary: This paper introduces the KIT-FISTO model, a newly developed risk stratification decision tree designed to predict obstructive acute kidney injury (AKI) in the emergency department setting.
articles.summary: This paper introduces the KIT-FISTO model, a newly developed risk stratification decision tree designed to predict obstructive acute kidney injury (AKI) in the emergency department setting. The study utilized a retrospective derivation and internal validation cohort approach on adult patients presenting with AKI of any KDIGO stage. The model stratifies risk based on clinical presentation; specifically, patients reporting lumbar, flank, or hypogastric pain are categorized as 'high risk,' showing an associated obstructive AKI risk around 55%. A 'moderate risk' group is defined for those without acute pain but with specific histories such as prior urinary tract surgery or abdominal cancer. The authors note that the model achieved high sensitivity for the 'low risk' category, suggesting its utility in ruling out significant obstruction.

articles.title: Real-time identification of aetiology in patients able to undergo transoesophageal echocardiography with non-traumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest in China: a prospective, single-centre exploratory study
articles.takeaway: TEE appears to be a rapid and feasible tool for identifying suspected causes during CPR in NT-OHCA patients, with acute aortic dissection being a key finding. However, remember that this data comes from a single center without a control group, so do not change your standard resuscitation algorithm based on this alone. It remains a valuable adjunct for guiding immediate interventions when available.
articles.shortSummary: This single-center, prospective study explored the utility of transoesophageal echocardiography (TEE) for identifying reversible causes in patients who experienced non-traumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (NT-OHCA)…
articles.summary: This single-center, prospective study explored the utility of transoesophageal echocardiography (TEE) for identifying reversible causes in patients who experienced non-traumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (NT-OHCA) and were able to undergo the procedure. The authors reported that TEE successfully provided diagnostic images in all 43 eligible patients examined, with a median door-to-TEE time of 13 minutes. They found that reversible etiologies were identified in 39% of cases, with acute aortic dissection being the most frequently diagnosed condition at 50%. While TEE guided interventions in over half of the identified cases, the study noted that there was no significant difference in both return of spontaneous circulation or 28-day survival rates when comparing patients whose cause was identified versus those where it was not. Crucially, the authors cautioned that due to the lack of a comparator group, further controlled investigation is necessary before drawing definitive conclusions about its role.

editions.headline: Fluid Wars, Late Stroke Care, and Outpatient PE Pathways
editions.summary:
The debate over initial resuscitation in septic shock continues to generate conflicting signals. The ARISE-FLUIDS trial suggests that rigid adherence to either aggressive fluid loading or immediate vasopressor use may not confer a survival advantage when comparing the two strategies head-to-head.

Shifting focus across acuity levels, we see practical shifts in management pathways. For acute ischemic stroke presenting significantly past 24 hours, advanced imaging combined with careful patient selection might still justify endovascular intervention to improve functional outcomes, though caution remains warranted due to mixed overall data. Furthermore, for low-risk patients presenting with acute PE managed on DOACs, the evidence suggests that inpatient admission may not confer a survival benefit over robust outpatient management.

Beyond resuscitation and thromboembolism, specialized tools are emerging: bedside cranial ultrasound is proving invaluable in differentiating intracranial collections in pediatric trauma, while TEE shows promise for rapidly diagnosing etiologies following non-traumatic OHCA. These varied findings—from refining the initial fluid bolus to optimizing post-discharge care—underscore that today's reading set demands a highly nuanced approach, prioritizing local protocols and patient status over sweeping algorithmic mandates.
editions.notes: Today’s reads emphasize using high-yield data points to refine existing pathways rather than overhaul them entirely.

articles.title: To squeeze or not to squeeze. The ARISE-FLUIDS trial
articles.takeaway: The ARISE-FLUIDS trial suggests caution regarding rigid adherence to either early, restricted-fluid vasopressors or high-volume resuscitation. At the bedside, remember that current data do not support a clear advantage for initiating vasopressors immediately with limited fluids versus using higher fluid loads first in septic shock. Always consider the patient's hemodynamic status and institutional protocols when titrating initial therapy.
articles.shortSummary: This review focuses on the ARISE-FLUIDS trial, which directly compared two distinct resuscitation strategies in septic shock patients presenting to the emergency department: one group received early vasopressors alongsi…
articles.summary: This review focuses on the ARISE-FLUIDS trial, which directly compared two distinct resuscitation strategies in septic shock patients presenting to the emergency department: one group received early vasopressors alongside restricted fluid administration, while the other received higher fluid volumes with later initiation of vasopressors. The primary finding reported is that neither of these management paradigms demonstrated a superior rate of survival at day 90 when compared against each other. This suggests that current resuscitation guidelines advocating for either aggressive initial fluid loading or immediate vasopressor use might lack definitive evidence supporting one over the other in this specific setting.

articles.title: Endovascular intervention for acute stroke in the very late window: A meta-analysis of 90-day mRS and procedural outcomes
articles.takeaway: For patients presenting with ischemic stroke significantly past 24 hours, consider EVT only after rigorous patient selection using advanced imaging to identify candidates most likely to benefit. While improved functional outcomes and reduced mortality are suggested in select groups, remember that the overall data is mixed, so proceed with caution and weigh potential risks against anticipated gains.
articles.shortSummary: This meta-analysis synthesized data regarding the use of Endovascular Therapy (EVT) for acute ischemic stroke presenting in very late windows, specifically beyond the standard 24-hour timeframe.
articles.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized data regarding the use of Endovascular Therapy (EVT) for acute ischemic stroke presenting in very late windows, specifically beyond the standard 24-hour timeframe. The authors concluded that when applied judiciously to select patients, EVT can indeed confer benefits by improving functional outcomes and reducing all-cause mortality. However, the analysis also stressed that patient selection is paramount, suggesting that a blanket application of this intervention is not supported by the data. Overall, while there are signals of benefit in specific cohorts, the overall evidence base presents mixed results across various outcome measures.

articles.title: Association of Serum Lactate with Outcome After Pediatric Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest: A Secondary Analysis of the Therapeutic Hypothermia After Pediatric Cardiac Arrest (THAPCA) Trial
articles.takeaway: If you encounter a child who has experienced out-of-hospital cardiac arrest and achieves ROSC, consider drawing lactate levels within the first six hours. Higher values appear associated with worse long-term outcomes, suggesting it might help stratify risk early on. However, this is an observational association from a secondary analysis, so don't rely on it as a standalone predictor.
articles.shortSummary: This secondary analysis re-examined the THAPCA-OH trial data specifically focusing on serum lactate levels following pediatric out-of-hospital cardiac arrest.
articles.summary: This secondary analysis re-examined the THAPCA-OH trial data specifically focusing on serum lactate levels following pediatric out-of-hospital cardiac arrest. The core finding suggests that elevated lactate concentrations measured within the first six hours after return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) correlate with poorer one-year outcomes in this population. This points toward lactate having potential utility as an adjunct biomarker for early neuroprognostication and predicting mortality risk following pediatric resuscitation. While promising, it is important to remember this analysis was derived from a specific trial cohort.

articles.title: Rocuronium vs Succinylcholine for RSI: Awareness, Paralysis, and Post-Intubation Sedation
articles.takeaway: When choosing a paralytic for RSI, be mindful that while rocuronium may delay optimal post-intubation sedation compared to succinylcholine, the magnitude of this difference is clinically debatable. Regardless of agent choice, always plan ahead for robust post-intubation analgesia and sedation protocols; don't assume adequate spontaneous recovery or readiness for advanced airway management.
articles.shortSummary: This review summarizes a retrospective study comparing the use of rocuronium versus succinylcholine during Rapid Sequence Intubation (RSI) in the emergency department setting, focusing on post-intubation sedation and an…
articles.summary: This review summarizes a retrospective study comparing the use of rocuronium versus succinylcholine during Rapid Sequence Intubation (RSI) in the emergency department setting, focusing on post-intubation sedation and analgesia needs. The authors noted that while rocuronium appeared associated with delayed initiation of post-intubation sedation and analgesia compared to succinylcholine, they cautioned that the absolute median differences observed were quite small, leading to uncertainty regarding true clinical significance. A key point raised is the concern surrounding potential awareness during paralysis when utilizing longer-acting agents like rocuronium. Given these findings, the overall recommendation leans toward proactively planning for adequate post-intubation sedation and analgesia before performing RSI, particularly when using agents with prolonged action.

articles.title: Critical care delivery models in emergency departments: a systematic review of the literature and meta-analysis of related outcome effects
articles.takeaway: When considering organizational flow, evidence points toward dedicated critical care areas (like an ED-ICU) potentially improving outcomes by lowering rates of both ICU transfer and total hospital days. While this meta-analysis is encouraging, remember that implementation requires significant resource allocation and staffing adjustments; these benefits are relative to the models compared in the review.
articles.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesized existing literature on various organizational models for delivering critical care within the emergency department setting, culminating in a meta-analysis of outcome effects.
articles.summary: This systematic review synthesized existing literature on various organizational models for delivering critical care within the emergency department setting, culminating in a meta-analysis of outcome effects. The authors categorized several delivery approaches, with dedicated critical care areas, such as an ED-ICU setup, being the most frequently studied model. The quantitative analysis specifically compared these dedicated units against other established CC-ED delivery models. The key finding suggests that establishing a dedicated critical care zone within the ED setting may confer benefits by reducing both the rate of subsequent ICU admission and overall hospital length of stay for patients presenting with acute critical illness in the department.

articles.title: Infant with seizure
articles.takeaway: When evaluating an infant post-trauma with concerning signs and fontanelle tension, performing cranial ultrasound is valuable for characterizing intracranial collections. Look specifically for Doppler evidence like displaced subarachnoid vessels or a negative cortical vein sign to help distinguish between SDH, SAH, and hygromas at the bedside. Remember that these findings are suggestive but do not replace definitive neuroimaging.
articles.shortSummary: This case report highlights the utility of bedside cranial ultrasonography in evaluating an infant presenting after trauma with concerning neurological signs, specifically irritability and tonic posturing following a fa…
articles.summary: This case report highlights the utility of bedside cranial ultrasonography in evaluating an infant presenting after trauma with concerning neurological signs, specifically irritability and tonic posturing following a fall. The patient presented with physical exam findings including ecchymoses and a tense anterior fontanelle. Ultrasound was instrumental in differentiating between potential intracranial collections, demonstrating bilateral heterogeneous, multilayered fluid collections. Crucially, the study notes that color Doppler imaging revealed displacement of subarachnoid vessels away from the cortical surface within these collections, along with an absence of traversing vessels, findings highly suggestive of subdural hematomas rather than a subarachnoid hemorrhage or hygroma. This underscores how advanced bedside ultrasound can refine the diagnosis in acute pediatric head trauma.

articles.title: ACMT Toxicology Visual Pearl – Black Vomit, Big Problem
articles.takeaway: When encountering a patient with black emesis and a garlicky odor, immediately consider phosphide poisoning from rodenticides, even if initial vitals are reassuring. Be prepared for rapid decompensation into shock due to cellular energy failure. Prompt supportive care focusing on decontamination and advanced life support is paramount.
articles.shortSummary: This visual pearl highlights the critical toxidrome associated with ingesting zinc or aluminum phosphide rodenticides, which are often sold as black pellets.
articles.summary: This visual pearl highlights the critical toxidrome associated with ingesting zinc or aluminum phosphide rodenticides, which are often sold as black pellets. Ingestion leads to a rapid onset of symptoms characterized by nausea and vomiting dark emesis that has a distinct garlicky or fishy odor. The underlying mechanism involves the release of phosphine gas, which is highly toxic because it inhibits mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation. Clinically, this presentation can be misleading; patients may initially appear stable before rapidly deteriorating into profound cardiovascular collapse. Recognizing this specific constellation of symptoms and the source material is key to prompt management.

articles.title: Predicting Echocardiography Findings in Adults Presenting to the Emergency Department With Syncope: An External Validation of the ROMEO Score
articles.takeaway: For low-risk patients presenting with syncope, remember that a zero ROMEO score suggests a very high likelihood that their TTE will be negative for significant findings. This tool can help triage which patients genuinely need an echo versus those who might benefit from alternative workups or observation. However, always interpret this in the context of the patient's overall clinical picture, as it is not a standalone diagnostic determinant.
articles.shortSummary: This paper presents an external validation of the ROMEO score, a tool designed to predict whether a patient presenting to the emergency department with syncope is likely to have significant findings on a Transthoracic E…
articles.summary: This paper presents an external validation of the ROMEO score, a tool designed to predict whether a patient presenting to the emergency department with syncope is likely to have significant findings on a Transthoracic Echocardiogram (TTE). The authors report that the score demonstrates strong predictive performance across different cohorts. Specifically, achieving a zero ROMEO score was associated with a very high Negative Predictive Value of 98.6%. This suggests that patients scoring zero are highly unlikely to harbor clinically significant structural heart abnormalities detectable by TTE. Therefore, the score offers a method to stratify risk and potentially guide the ordering of this diagnostic test in the acute syncope setting.

articles.title: Outcomes of Acute PE Treated With DOACs in the Veterans Affairs Health System: A Retrospective Cohort Study
articles.takeaway: For low-risk acute PE patients (sPESI 0 or 1) managed on DOACs, inpatient admission does not appear to improve 30-day mortality over discharge. This supports aggressively considering and implementing robust outpatient pathways when the patient has no other compelling reason for admission. Be mindful that this is a retrospective analysis, so these findings should guide protocol refinement rather than replacing established guidelines entirely.
articles.shortSummary: This retrospective cohort study analyzed the 30-day mortality outcomes in a large U.S.
articles.summary: This retrospective cohort study analyzed the 30-day mortality outcomes in a large U.S. population presenting with acute pulmonary embolism (PE) who were managed with direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs). The authors stratified their analysis based on the Wells' criteria equivalent, the sPESI score. Notably, for patients categorized as low risk, specifically those scoring 0 or 1 on the sPESI, the data indicated that receiving inpatient hospitalization did not confer a survival benefit compared to being managed in an outpatient setting. Given that two-thirds of these low-risk patients were still admitted despite this finding, the study strongly suggests a potential shift toward expanding appropriate outpatient management protocols for acute PE.

articles.title: Status epilepticus in patients with known epilepsy presenting to the emergency department: Predictors and mortality risk
articles.takeaway: Given that SE is relatively common in the ED setting for epilepsy patients and carries a significant mortality risk, consider proactively stratifying risk rather than waiting for overt signs. Pay extra attention to older individuals or those with concurrent metabolic issues or suspected CNS infection, as these groups appear to be at higher risk of decompensation.

articles.summary: This review looked at the frequency and predictors of status epilepticus (SE) specifically within a cohort of epilepsy patients presenting to the emergency department in Malaysia. The authors found that SE was quite common, occurring in nearly one-quarter of this population, and critically, it demonstrated a strong association with increased in-hospital mortality. This suggests that simply having an epilepsy diagnosis does not negate the need for vigilance regarding acute neurological decompensation upon ED presentation. The findings are particularly useful because they highlight specific subgroups—namely older patients or those presenting with concurrent metabolic derangements or suspected central nervous system infections—who warrant heightened suspicion and proactive risk stratification.

articles.title: Risk of obstructive acute kidney injury: derivation and internal validation of a risk stratification tree
articles.takeaway: When managing AKI in the ED, consider using this risk stratification tool if available, particularly noting that acute flank/lumbar pain places a patient in a high-risk category with an estimated 55% chance of obstruction. Remember that the model's utility is currently limited to internal validation; therefore, do not rely on it for definitive management decisions without external confirmation.

The study utilized a retrospective derivation and internal validation cohort approach on patients presenting with AKI of any KDIGO stage. The model stratifies risk based on clinical presentation; specifically, patients reporting lumbar, flank, or hypogastric pain are categorized as 'high risk,' showing an associated obstructive AKI risk around 55%. A 'moderate risk' group is defined for those without acute pain but possessing specific histories such as prior urinary tract surgery or abdominal cancer. The authors note that the model was derived and validated internally, suggesting its utility while cautioning that external and prospective validation remains necessary before clinical implementation.

articles.title: Airway Foreign Bodies
articles.takeaway: Maintain a persistent suspicion for an AFB in any patient presenting with concerning respiratory symptoms, as subtle signs are common. For adults, initial evaluation should likely involve flexible bronchoscopy, but be prepared to escalate to rigid bronchoscopy if the object appears large or deeply lodged. Remember that management hinges on the size and location of the suspected foreign material.
articles.shortSummary: This review provides a thorough overview of airway foreign bodies (AFBs), emphasizing that these can be potentially life-threatening emergencies requiring high index of suspicion regardless of the initial presentation.
articles.summary: This review provides a thorough overview of airway foreign bodies (AFBs), emphasizing that these can be potentially life-threatening emergencies requiring high index of suspicion regardless of the initial presentation. The authors note that while AFBs affect various demographics, there is a tendency for presentations to be subtle and thus prone to misdiagnosis. Management strategies are somewhat dependent on the patient population; flexible bronchoscopy is generally recommended as the first line in most adult cases. However, rigid bronchoscopy retains its critical role when dealing with larger or more proximal obstructing objects. Understanding the underlying pathophysiology and potential complications is key for comprehensive management.

articles.title: Real-time identification of aetiology in patients able to undergo transoesophageal echocardiography with non-traumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest in China: a prospective, single-centre exploratory study
articles.takeaway: TEE appears to be a valuable adjunct for rapidly identifying suspected etiologies in NT-OHCA patients when available, with acute aortic dissection being a key consideration. However, given the lack of a comparator group and its exploratory nature, do not rely on these findings alone to change standard resuscitation protocols; it remains a tool requiring further controlled validation.

The authors reported that TEE successfully provided diagnostic images in all 43 patients who underwent the exam, suggesting its feasibility during active resuscitation efforts. They found that reversible etiologies were identified in 39% of cases, with acute aortic dissection being the most frequently diagnosed cause at 50%. While TEE guided interventions were performed in over half of the identified cases, the study noted limitations due to its single-center design and lack of a comparator group for definitive conclusions. Overall, it suggests TEE is a rapid tool for initial diagnostic suspicion during CPR.

articles.title: PulmCrit: Is oseltamivir assassinating ICU patients?
articles.takeaway: Given the preliminary and highly concerning data suggesting a 98.3% increased mortality risk, exercise extreme caution before initiating oseltamivir in critically ill patients. While further review of the full dataset is warranted, these initial findings strongly argue against routine use in this population until more robust analyses are available. Do not change established protocols based solely on this preliminary readout.
articles.shortSummary: The REMAP-CAP adaptive platform trial has released preliminary data regarding the use of oseltamivir in critically ill patients, and the findings are quite alarming.
articles.summary: The REMAP-CAP adaptive platform trial has released preliminary data regarding the use of oseltamivir in critically ill patients, and the findings are quite alarming. The primary analysis presented suggests a substantial risk associated with administering this antiviral to those in the ICU setting. Specifically, the reported likelihood of increased mortality linked to oseltamivir was calculated at 98.3%. Given these preliminary results, it strongly implies that current guidelines regarding routine use of oseltamivir in severe respiratory illness need immediate re-evaluation. Clinicians should treat this data with caution but recognize its potential weight for guiding immediate patient management.

articles.title: Shift Pearls: Anatomic Urinary Obstruction and Septic Shock
articles.takeaway: Keep a high index of suspicion for urological causes of shock in any patient presenting with unexplained hypotension or oliguria. While specific management details are pending, remember that obstruction itself can precipitate or worsen septic insult by causing local tissue ischemia and contributing to systemic inflammatory response syndrome. Be prepared to rapidly assess for urinary tract pathology alongside standard sepsis workup.
articles.shortSummary: This 'Shift Pearls' installment addresses the critical intersection of anatomic urinary obstruction and septic shock, a high-yield topic for emergency practice.
articles.summary: This 'Shift Pearls' installment addresses the critical intersection of anatomic urinary obstruction and septic shock, a high-yield topic for emergency practice. While the provided excerpt is merely an announcement of the topic rather than detailing specific findings or management algorithms, it signals that practical pearls are forthcoming regarding urological issues in this critically ill patient population. The underlying clinical concern here is recognizing how acute urinary retention or obstruction can complicate the picture of septic shock, potentially contributing to or mimicking other sources of sepsis. Given the complexity, managing fluid status and source control becomes paramount when these two systems overlap.

articles.title: Case Report: When Sore Throat Is More Than Just a Pain in the Neck
articles.takeaway: If a patient develops a sore throat persisting beyond 24 hours post-intubation, don't just treat it as routine pharyngitis. Perform a meticulous oropharyngeal exam specifically searching for white necrotic tips on the uvula, as this suggests potential uvular necrosis. Remember that while the diagnosis is clinical, prompt recognition can prevent airway compromise.
articles.shortSummary: This case report highlights that persistent postoperative sore throat, especially following intubation, warrants consideration of rare but serious complications like uvular necrosis.
articles.summary: This case report highlights that persistent postoperative sore throat, especially following intubation, warrants consideration of rare but serious complications like uvular necrosis. The authors emphasize that the diagnosis remains primarily clinical, necessitating a detailed history and physical examination rather than solely relying on imaging or microbiology cultures. A key diagnostic step involves a careful oropharyngeal inspection specifically looking for white necrotic tips on the uvula. This presentation serves as an important reminder to the emergency physician that persistent pharyngitis symptoms might be masking a more ominous underlying process related to airway management.

articles.title: Frailty stratification in the emergency department using the triage frailty and comorbidity tool
articles.takeaway: Consider using the TFC Tool during triage for low-acuity patients as part of your standard workflow; it appears to improve timely assessment for frail individuals without compromising overall acuity flow. Remember that a positive score suggests higher risk, which is supported by its association with hospital admission. Don't let this replace core acuity assessment, but use it as an adjunct screen.
articles.shortSummary: This paper evaluates the utility of integrating the Triage Frailty and Comorbidity (TFC) Tool into routine triage processes for low-acuity patients within the emergency department setting.
articles.summary: This paper evaluates the utility of integrating the Triage Frailty and Comorbidity (TFC) Tool into routine triage processes for low-acuity patients within the emergency department setting. The core finding suggests that incorporating this tool helps identify vulnerable, frail individuals who might otherwise be overlooked in standard acuity sorting. Importantly, the authors report that using the TFC Tool successfully reduced waiting times to medical assessment specifically for these high-risk, low-acuity patients without disrupting the established system of acuity prioritization. Furthermore, a positive TFC score was independently associated with increased odds of subsequent hospital admission, suggesting it's a useful prognostic marker in this population.

articles.title: Validation of the 2022 German trauma team activation criteria: a national registry study with focus on geriatric-specific modifiers
articles.takeaway: When managing elderly trauma patients, remember that incorporating geriatric-specific modifiers into your triage assessment is supported by evidence showing improved alignment with actual mortality risk. These updated criteria should guide your decision on activating higher levels of trauma resources. Be mindful that while this improves prediction, further work is needed to fully quantify the impact on overtriage rates at the bedside.
articles.shortSummary: This national registry study validates the updated 2022 German trauma team activation (TTA) criteria, paying special attention to how geriatric-specific modifiers impact resource utilization and patient outcomes.
articles.summary: This national registry study validates the updated 2022 German trauma team activation (TTA) criteria, paying special attention to how geriatric-specific modifiers impact resource utilization and patient outcomes. The authors found that incorporating these modifications significantly improves the alignment between initial triage assessments and actual observed mortality risk across the cohort. Specifically, the enhanced predictive accuracy noted among older patients suggests these updated guidelines are clinically superior for guiding trauma system activation decisions. While the findings strongly support the utility of the revised criteria, the paper also points toward future work needed to quantify the direct implications regarding overtriage rates and overall resource allocation efficiency.

articles.title: No Pause, No Problem? Using Doppler Ultrasound to Detect ROSC Without Pausing Compressions for Pulse Check
articles.takeaway: If you are concerned about pulse checks compromising CPR quality, remember that Doppler assessment of femoral flow during compressions offers a potential way to confirm ROSC without interruption. While promising for maintaining high-quality chest compressions, this remains an area needing further validation before it changes standard protocol; do not rely on this alone until more robust evidence emerges.
articles.shortSummary: This review tackles the persistent issue of interruptions during resuscitation, specifically focusing on how traditional pulse checks disrupt high-quality CPR.
articles.summary: This review tackles the persistent issue of interruptions during resuscitation, specifically focusing on how traditional pulse checks disrupt high-quality CPR. The core concept explored is leveraging femoral arterial Doppler ultrasound waveforms obtained *during* active compressions to detect signs of Return of Spontaneous Circulation (ROSC). The authors evaluate the diagnostic accuracy of detecting pulsatility and anterograde flow signals via Doppler compared to established methods for confirming true cardiac activity. If validated, this technique could represent a significant workflow improvement by allowing continuous monitoring without necessitating pauses in chest compressions, thereby maximizing CPR quality.

articles.title: Patterns of emergency department visits for suicidal attempts associated with toxic ingestion: A retrospective cross-sectional study
articles.takeaway: When managing an overdose presentation involving multiple agents, especially CNS depressants, prompt toxicology consultation is non-negotiable for optimizing care pathways. Remember that the management extends beyond antidotes to include comprehensive risk assessment and coordination with mental health services. Be mindful that polypharmacy complicates everything, so a thorough toxicological workup is key.

articles.summary: This retrospective analysis characterizing intentional self-poisoning presentations in Israel highlights a significant trend: most ingestions are medication-related, often involving polypharmacy that includes central nervous system depressants. The authors emphasize that the complexity of these cases necessitates early and robust toxicology consultation to effectively guide management strategies and improve patient outcomes. Beyond immediate stabilization, the discussion underscores the broader role of medical toxicologists in not only acute care but also in surveillance, prevention efforts, and addressing the holistic psycho-social needs of patients presenting with self-harm risk. It paints a picture of these ED visits as complex encounters requiring multidisciplinary input.

editions.headline: Fluid Wars and Late-Stage Stroke: What's Shifting in Resuscitation?
editions.summary:
The resuscitation landscape remains fluid, perhaps literally. The ARISE-FLUIDS data offers a sobering message for septic shock management: the debate between aggressive early vasopressors with restricted fluids versus more liberal initial boluses appears unlikely to shift 90-day survival. This suggests that rigid adherence to one sequence over another might be less impactful than tailoring care based on real-time hemodynamic targets.

Elsewhere, stroke care continues to push boundaries. For those presenting significantly past the standard 24-hour window, a meta-analysis suggests Endovascular Therapy (EVT) *can* confer benefits, but only if patient selection is highly refined—the benefit isn't universal across all late presenters. Meanwhile, in pediatric OHCA, lactate levels measured within six hours post-ROSC are emerging as a potentially useful adjunct biomarker for risk stratification.

Beyond the critical pathways, we see practical shifts: evidence supports establishing dedicated critical care zones within the ED to improve outcomes, and for low-risk acute PE patients managed on DOACs, routine inpatient admission may not offer a survival advantage over outpatient management. Keep these nuanced findings in mind as you refine your algorithms this week.
editions.notes: The message today is synthesis: use emerging data to adjust protocols, but don't discard established principles.
